lessons.title: Introduction to Matter in Daily Life

concepts.definition: Anything that has mass and takes up space.
concepts.key_fact: Everything around us that takes up space is matter.

templates: What do we call anything that {verb_phrase}?
In science, what is defined as something that {verb_phrase}?
Which term describes any substance that {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: has mass and takes up space
occupies space and possesses mass

concepts.rationale: Matter is fundamentally defined as any physical substance that occupies space (volume) and possesses mass.

concepts.definition: The amount of material in an object.
concepts.key_fact: Heavier objects generally contain more mass than lighter ones.

templates: What scientific term refers to the {noun_phrase}?
The {noun_phrase} is known as its what?

noun_phrase: amount of material in an object
total amount of matter an object contains

concepts.rationale: Mass measures the actual amount of matter in an object, distinguishing it from volume (space taken up) and weight (the pull of gravity on that matter).

concepts.definition: The physical room or space an object occupies.
concepts.key_fact: All states of matter, including invisible gases, have volume.

templates: What is the specific term for the {noun_phrase}?
Which measurement describes the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: physical room an object occupies
total amount of three-dimensional space an object takes up

concepts.rationale: Volume refers specifically to the three-dimensional space enclosed by or occupied by any state of matter.

concepts.definition: Things that do not have mass or take up space.
concepts.key_fact: Light, heat, and sound are examples of non-matter.

templates: What do we call things that {verb_phrase}?
If an item is proven to {verb_phrase}, what category does it belong to?

verb_phrase: not have mass or take up any physical space
be forms of energy rather than physical substances

concepts.rationale: Non-matter refers to things like light, heat, and sound, which are forms of energy and do not possess physical mass or volume.

concepts.definition: Everyday objects found at home that have mass and volume.
concepts.key_fact: Chairs, beds, and water are all matter found at home.

templates: Everyday objects like chairs and beds are examples of {noun_phrase}?
Which term best describes physical objects we use daily that are categorized as {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: matter found at home
common matter used inside a house

concepts.rationale: Physical objects in our daily living spaces that have mass and take up space are considered household matter.

concepts.definition: School supplies and furniture that have mass and volume.
concepts.key_fact: Desks, whiteboards, and pencils are matter in the classroom.

templates: School supplies and desks are examples of {noun_phrase}?
Pencils and whiteboards represent what kind of {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: matter in the classroom
matter found inside a school environment

concepts.rationale: Any physical object in an educational setting that has mass and takes up space qualifies as classroom matter.

concepts.definition: Characteristics of matter that can be described using our senses.
concepts.key_fact: Color, size, shape, and texture are observable properties.

templates: What do we call the {noun_phrase}?
Color and texture are prime examples of {noun_phrase}.

noun_phrase: characteristics of matter described using our natural senses
traits of a physical object we can see, feel, or smell

concepts.rationale: Observable properties are physical traits that do not require testing to be identified, such as color and texture.

concepts.definition: The tiny, invisible pieces that make up all matter.
concepts.key_fact: Even though a solid looks like one piece, it is made of millions of tiny particles.

templates: All matter is made up of {noun_phrase}?
What are the {noun_phrase} that form all physical objects?

noun_phrase: tiny, invisible pieces called particles
microscopic building blocks called particles

concepts.rationale: All matter, regardless of its state, is fundamentally composed of tiny units called particles.

concepts.definition: Matter that is alive, such as plants, animals, and humans.
concepts.key_fact: Living matter grows, needs energy, and takes up space.

templates: What do we call matter that {verb_phrase}?
Humans and trees fall into the category of matter that {verb_phrase}. What is this called?

verb_phrase: is biologically alive
naturally grows and requires a food energy source

concepts.rationale: Matter is divided into living (biological organisms) and non-living (inanimate objects). Both have mass and volume, but living matter requires energy to grow.

concepts.definition: Understanding matter helps us interact with the world and solve daily problems.
concepts.key_fact: We choose different types of matter for different jobs, like using hard matter for building.

templates: Understanding the {noun_phrase} helps us solve real-world engineering problems.
Choosing the right physical material for a specific job demonstrates the {noun_phrase}.

noun_phrase: importance of understanding matter
value in studying matter's unique properties

concepts.rationale: By understanding the properties of different types of matter, humans can safely and effectively build structures, create tools, and solve daily problems.

concepts.definition: A state of matter with a fixed shape and a fixed volume.
concepts.key_fact: Solids do not change their shape when moved from one place to another.

templates: Which state of matter is defined by having a {noun_phrase}?
An object that has a {noun_phrase} is classified as what?

noun_phrase: fixed shape and fixed volume
definite shape that cannot easily flow

concepts.rationale: A solid is a state of matter characterized by a rigid structure that maintains both its shape and its volume.

concepts.definition: A property where an object keeps its original form and does not flow.
concepts.key_fact: Unlike liquids and gases, solids naturally have a definite shape.

templates: Because a solid {verb_phrase}, we say it has a definite shape.
Which property explains why a wooden block {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: keeps its original form and does not flow
maintains its exact physical form regardless of its container

concepts.rationale: Definite shape means the matter strongly resists changing its form, a key identifier of a solid.

concepts.definition: A property where an object takes up a specific, unchanging amount of space.
concepts.key_fact: Even if you move a solid to a bigger container, its volume stays the exact same.

templates: When an object is observed to have a {noun_phrase}, it is said to have a definite volume.
Which term describes the fact that a solid takes up a {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: specific, unchanging amount of space
fixed amount of measurable space

concepts.rationale: Definite volume ensures that the physical space occupied by the object cannot be compressed or expanded under normal conditions.

concepts.definition: Particles are tightly packed together and only vibrate in place.
concepts.key_fact: This tight packing is the reason why solids are hard and keep their shape.

templates: In a solid, how are the particles {verb_phrase}?
Which phrase best explains how solid particles are {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: arranged to hold the object's shape
positioned internally to make the matter firm

concepts.rationale: The microscopic arrangement of particles in a solid is highly dense and rigid, preventing them from sliding past one another.

concepts.definition: A property of a solid describing its resistance to being scratched, bent, or dented.
concepts.key_fact: Diamond is an example of a solid with an extremely high level of hardness.

templates: What property measures a solid's {noun_phrase}?
If a solid shows high {noun_phrase}, what observable property does it possess?

noun_phrase: resistance to being scratched or dented
ability to withstand surface pressure without getting scratched

concepts.rationale: Hardness defines how well a solid material resists external forces that attempt to alter its surface.

concepts.definition: How the surface of a solid feels, such as rough or smooth.
concepts.key_fact: We can observe a solid's texture primarily by using our sense of touch.

templates: What do we call the {noun_phrase}?
The words 'rough' and 'smooth' are used to describe what {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: observation of how the surface of an object feels
physical property detailing the way an object feels when touched

concepts.rationale: Texture is an observable physical property interpreted primarily through tactile sensation.

concepts.definition: Recognizing objects like books, rocks, and ice as being in the solid state.
concepts.key_fact: If you can hold an object and it doesn't spill or float away, it is usually a solid.

templates: Books, rocks, and ice are all examples of {noun_phrase}?
When you identify physical objects that keep their shape, you are identifying {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: solid matter
matter existing purely in the solid state

concepts.rationale: Everyday objects that can be held and do not conform to their container are classified as solids.

concepts.definition: The ability of a solid to maintain its form when moved or placed in different containers.
concepts.key_fact: A wooden block placed inside a round bowl will still remain a block.

templates: What property explains a solid's ability to {verb_phrase}?
Which term refers to a solid's unique capacity to {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: maintain its geometric form in a differently shaped container
keep its exact rigid shape when moved across surfaces

concepts.rationale: Shape retention is directly tied to the strong bonds between the solid's tightly packed particles.

concepts.definition: The ability of some solids to bend without breaking.
concepts.key_fact: Rubber and certain plastics are examples of highly flexible solids.

templates: What physical property describes a solid's ability to {verb_phrase}?
Rubber demonstrates which distinct property when it is able to {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: bend considerably without breaking
flex and deform without snapping in half

concepts.rationale: Flexibility allows a solid's structure to temporarily deform under stress without suffering permanent failure or breaking.

concepts.definition: The tendency of a solid to break, snap, or shatter easily when a force is applied.
concepts.key_fact: Glass and dry clay are common examples of brittle solids.

templates: What is the property that causes a solid to {verb_phrase}?
Which term describes a material's high tendency to {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: break or shatter easily under pressure
snap rapidly when a sudden force is applied

concepts.rationale: Brittleness is the opposite of flexibility; brittle materials cannot absorb much energy before fracturing.

concepts.definition: A state of matter that flows.
concepts.key_fact: Liquids can be poured easily and do not have a fixed shape.

templates: Which state of matter is defined by having a {noun_phrase}?
A substance characterized by its {noun_phrase} is classified as what?

noun_phrase: flowing nature rather than a fixed rigid structure
ability to be poured easily without a fixed shape

concepts.rationale: Liquids are defined by their ability to flow freely and conform to containers, distinguishing them from rigid solids.

concepts.definition: A property where matter takes the exact shape of whatever container it is in.
concepts.key_fact: If you pour a liquid from a round bowl into a square box, it becomes square.

templates: Because water changes its form based on its container, we say it has the {noun_phrase}?
Which property explains why a liquid possesses the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: ability to take the exact shape of its container
tendency to adapt to the shape of whatever holds it

concepts.rationale: Because the particles in a liquid can slide past one another, they succumb to gravity and fill the shape of their container.

concepts.definition: A property where the amount of liquid stays the same regardless of the container's shape.
concepts.key_fact: Pouring 100mL of water into a taller glass still leaves you with exactly 100mL of water.

templates: Even when poured into different glasses, a liquid takes up a {noun_phrase}. What is this called?
What property ensures that liquid occupies a {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: specific, unchanging amount of total space
constant and measurable amount of space

concepts.rationale: While a liquid changes shape, its particles cannot be easily compressed, meaning it maintains a constant, definite volume.

concepts.definition: Particles are close together but have enough room to slide past one another.
concepts.key_fact: This sliding movement is the reason why liquids are able to flow and be poured.

templates: In a liquid, how do the internal particles {verb_phrase}?
Which phrase best explains how particles {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: move to allow the substance to flow
behave when a liquid is actively being poured

concepts.rationale: The particles in a liquid have more energy than in a solid, allowing them to break rigid bonds and slide over each other.

concepts.definition: The ability of a liquid to pour and move continuously.
concepts.key_fact: Water flowing down a river is an example of a liquid's fluidity.

templates: What property describes a liquid's unique ability to {verb_phrase}?
Water demonstrates which fundamental property when it is able to {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: pour and move easily across a surface
flow continuously down a stream

concepts.rationale: Fluidity is the macroscopic result of liquid particles sliding past one another, allowing continuous movement.

concepts.definition: The thickness of a liquid or its resistance to flowing.
concepts.key_fact: Honey flows much slower than water because honey has a higher viscosity.

templates: What property measures a liquid's {noun_phrase}?
Which physical property is defined strictly as a material's {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: thickness or chemical resistance to flowing
slowness and internal friction when being poured

concepts.rationale: Viscosity determines how thick a liquid is; high viscosity liquids (like honey) resist flow more than low viscosity liquids (like water).

concepts.definition: Recognizing substances like water, juice, and oil as being in the liquid state.
concepts.key_fact: If you can pour an item and it splashes, it is most likely a liquid.

templates: Water, juice, and oil are all observable examples of {noun_phrase}?
When you identify substances that can easily be poured, you are identifying {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: liquid matter
substances physically in the liquid state

concepts.rationale: Everyday consumable and pourable items like juice and oil are practical classifications of the liquid state.

concepts.definition: Observing how liquids behave when transferred between containers.
concepts.key_fact: A liquid will continuously change its shape to match the new container as it is poured.

templates: When transferred between containers, what does a liquid continuously do to {verb_phrase}?
Observing a poured liquid shows that it will change shape to {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: match the geometric structure of the new container
fit snugly into the container it enters

concepts.rationale: Pouring demonstrates a liquid's fluidity and its defining characteristic of having no definite shape.

concepts.definition: How liquids settle to form a flat horizontal surface when at rest.
concepts.key_fact: No matter how you tilt a half-full cup, the top of the resting liquid will always settle flat.

templates: When at rest, what kind of surface does a liquid form to {verb_phrase}?
Gravity causes a resting liquid to create a flat surface to {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: level out evenly at the top
settle in a perfectly balanced manner

concepts.rationale: Due to gravity and fluidity, a liquid's particles will spread out until they form a completely flat, horizontal top layer when undisturbed.

concepts.definition: Observing what happens when two different liquids are combined.
concepts.key_fact: Some liquids mix perfectly together, while others, like oil and water, will separate.

templates: When two different liquids are mixed, they may uniquely {verb_phrase}. What are these possible outcomes?
Depending on the chemical makeup of the liquids, they will {verb_phrase}. What happens?

verb_phrase: mix perfectly together or separate completely into layers
blend seamlessly together or stubbornly stay apart

concepts.rationale: Liquid combinations depend on density and chemical polarity, resulting in either a homogeneous mixture or separated layers.

concepts.definition: A state of matter that expands to fill its space.
concepts.key_fact: Gases will spread out in all directions until they fill the container they are in.

templates: Which state of matter is defined as a substance that naturally {verb_phrase}?
A material that effortlessly {verb_phrase} is classified as what?

verb_phrase: expands to fill all of its available space
spreads out completely to fill its entire container

concepts.rationale: A gas is defined by having neither a fixed shape nor a fixed volume, allowing it to expand indefinitely to fill a container.

concepts.definition: A property where a gas takes the exact shape of its entire container.
concepts.key_fact: If you pump a gas into a star-shaped balloon, the gas becomes star-shaped.

templates: Because a gas changes its form to match a balloon, we say it has the {noun_phrase}?
Which structural property explains why a gas possesses the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: ability to take the exact shape of its entire container
trait of completely adapting to the shape of whatever holds it

concepts.rationale: Similar to liquids, gases flow and conform entirely to the shape of any container they are placed in.

concepts.definition: A property where a gas expands or compresses to fill available space.
concepts.key_fact: The exact same amount of gas can spread out to fill a large room or be squeezed into a tiny tank.

templates: Because gas does not take up a fixed amount of space, it possesses the {noun_phrase}. What is this property called?
What volume property ensures that a gas has the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: ability to naturally expand or compress to fill available space
capacity to drastically change its volume to fit the space it is given

concepts.rationale: Unlike solids and liquids, the distance between gas particles can be easily altered, meaning its volume is not fixed.

concepts.definition: Particles in a gas are spread far apart and move freely at high speeds.
concepts.key_fact: This free and rapid movement is why gases can spread out and fill a room so quickly.

templates: In a gas, how do the particles {verb_phrase}?
Which phrase best explains how gas particles {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: arrange themselves to be spread far apart and move freely
behave by becoming widely separated and moving quickly

concepts.rationale: Gas particles have the highest thermal energy of the three standard states, causing them to move chaotically and spread far apart.

concepts.definition: A property where many gases cannot be seen with the naked eye.
concepts.key_fact: Even though we cannot see the air around us, it is still made of physical matter.

templates: What descriptive property applies to a gas that {verb_phrase}?
Oxygen demonstrates which property when it {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: cannot be seen with the human naked eye
is completely invisible to human sight

concepts.rationale: Because gas particles are spread so thin and many do not absorb visible light, they appear completely invisible.

concepts.definition: Recognizing that the air we breathe is made of different gases mixed together.
concepts.key_fact: Air is not just one gas; it is mostly made of nitrogen and oxygen.

templates: What term describes the fact that the air we breathe {verb_phrase}?
The air around us is best described as a what, because it {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: is actually made of different gases combined
contains a complex combination of various gases

concepts.rationale: Earth's atmosphere is a mixture composed primarily of Nitrogen (78%) and Oxygen (21%).

concepts.definition: The ability to squeeze or force gas into a smaller space.
concepts.key_fact: You can squeeze a balloon full of air easily because gases are highly compressible.

templates: What property measures a gas's ability to {verb_phrase}?
Which physical property is defined as a material's capacity to {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: be squeezed physically into a much smaller space
be safely compressed into a tighter area

concepts.rationale: Because the particles in a gas are very far apart, they can easily be pushed closer together (compressed) by an outside force.

concepts.definition: Proving that gases are matter because they have weight and take up space.
concepts.key_fact: An inflated balloon weighs slightly more than a deflated balloon because the air inside has mass.

templates: When an inflated balloon is weighed, it shows that gases {verb_phrase}. What property do they have?
Observing that gases actively {verb_phrase} proves they have what property?

verb_phrase: weigh measurably more than empty space
add measurable physical weight to an object

concepts.rationale: Though very light, gases are composed of physical particles (atoms and molecules) which all possess a measurable mass.

concepts.definition: Understanding wind as moving air or gas from one place to another.
concepts.key_fact: We can feel wind on our skin and see it move trees, even if we cannot see the gas itself.

templates: When we experience {noun_phrase}, what specific weather phenomenon do we call it?
What is the common weather term used for a large volume of {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: moving air or gas
atmospheric air that is in fast motion

concepts.rationale: Wind is simply the bulk movement of the invisible gas mixture (air) that makes up the Earth's atmosphere.

concepts.definition: Recognizing common gases like oxygen, carbon dioxide, and helium.
concepts.key_fact: Helium is the specific gas used to make party balloons float.

templates: Oxygen, carbon dioxide, and helium are all observable examples of {noun_phrase}?
When you identify helium inside a floating balloon, you are identifying {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: gaseous matter
substances existing strictly in the gas state

concepts.rationale: Elements and compounds that naturally lack definite shape or volume at room temperature (like Helium and Oxygen) are identified as gases.

concepts.definition: Quantifying how much space a liquid takes up.
concepts.key_fact: We measure volume to know exactly how much liquid we have in a container.

templates: What do we precisely call the scientific process of {verb_phrase}?
In science, the mathematical act of {verb_phrase} is known as what?

verb_phrase: quantifying how much physical space a liquid takes up
finding out the exact numerical amount of space a liquid occupies

concepts.rationale: Measuring volume is the act of assigning a specific number and unit to the 3D space occupied by a substance.

concepts.definition: Tall, narrow scientific tools used for exact measurement of liquids.
concepts.key_fact: Because they are narrow, measuring cylinders give much more accurate readings than wide cups.

templates: Which specific scientific tools are categorized as {noun_phrase}?
What are the names of the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: tall, narrow tools used for exact liquid volume measurement
tall and thin glass containers used to accurately measure volume

concepts.rationale: Measuring cylinders (graduated cylinders) have a narrow diameter, meaning small changes in volume show as large vertical changes on the scale, increasing accuracy.

concepts.definition: Wide scientific tools used for approximate measurement and mixing.
concepts.key_fact: Beakers are great for stirring and mixing liquids because of their wide openings.

templates: Which scientific tools are broadly known as {noun_phrase}?
In a lab setting, {noun_phrase} are called what?

noun_phrase: wide scientific tools meant for approximate measurement and mixing
wide glass containers used mostly for mixing liquids together safely

concepts.rationale: Beakers have wide mouths that make pouring and stirring easy, but their broad scales make them poor tools for exact measurements.

concepts.definition: The standard unit used for small volumes of liquid.
concepts.key_fact: A standard medicine dropper usually holds about one milliliter of liquid.

templates: What is the correct metric unit to use when {verb_phrase}?
Scientists use what exact measurement unit for {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: measuring very small volumes of a liquid
recording tiny amounts of liquid like a few drops

concepts.rationale: Milliliters (mL) are a metric unit measuring 1/1000th of a liter, perfectly scaled for small, handheld amounts of liquid.

concepts.definition: The standard unit used for larger volumes of liquid.
concepts.key_fact: A large bottle of soda or a jug of water is usually measured in liters.

templates: What is the standard metric unit selected when {verb_phrase}?
When {verb_phrase}, what is the best base unit to use?

verb_phrase: measuring larger volumes of liquid like a big bottle
recording big, heavy amounts of water or juice

concepts.rationale: Liters (L) are the base metric unit for volume, appropriate for everyday larger quantities like beverages and buckets.

concepts.definition: The proper technique of reading liquid volume at eye level.
concepts.key_fact: Water naturally curves downward in a glass tube, so you must read the bottom of this curve.

templates: What do we officially call the technique of {verb_phrase}?
If a lab scientist is {verb_phrase}, what critical technique are they doing?

verb_phrase: reading the curved liquid volume at perfect eye level
looking closely at the bottom of the liquid curve at eye level

concepts.rationale: Because water molecules cling to glass (adhesion), a curved meniscus forms. You must read from the center (bottom) of the curve at eye level for accuracy.

concepts.definition: Knowing when to use milliliters (mL) versus liters (L) based on the amount of liquid.
concepts.key_fact: You would use mL for a spoon of medicine, but L for measuring a swimming pool.

templates: What broad scientific skill involves {verb_phrase}?
The mental process of successfully {verb_phrase} is called what?

verb_phrase: knowing exactly when to use mL versus L for a liquid
choosing the mathematically correct volume measurement unit based on size

concepts.rationale: Selecting the correct unit prevents massive numerical errors (e.g., measuring a pool as 500,000 mL is accurate but impractical compared to 500 L).

concepts.definition: Determining which container holds more or less liquid.
concepts.key_fact: A short, wide bowl might actually hold more liquid than a tall, skinny glass.

templates: What is the analytical process of {verb_phrase}?
Which broad term describes the careful action of {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: determining precisely which container holds more or less liquid
figuring out mathematically which glass has the larger amount of water

concepts.rationale: Because containers have different shapes (height vs width), humans must systematically compare volume numbers rather than trusting visual estimates.

concepts.definition: Why precise measurements matter in scientific experiments.
concepts.key_fact: Adding even a little bit too much liquid can completely ruin a science experiment.

templates: What overarching concept emphasizes the {noun_phrase}?
Scientists strive for what important standard regarding the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: need for precise volume measurements in science
absolute necessity of making sure the exact correct amount of liquid is used

concepts.rationale: Accuracy dictates how close a measured value is to the true value, which is vital for experiments to be repeatable and safe.

concepts.definition: The maximum volume a specific container can hold.
concepts.key_fact: Even if a 1-liter bottle is only half full, its total capacity is still 1 liter.

templates: What specific property identifies the {noun_phrase}?
Which technical term officially refers to the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: absolute maximum volume a specific container can hold
theoretical most liquid a container can fit inside before overflowing

concepts.rationale: Capacity refers to the potential internal volume of a container, not the actual volume of liquid currently inside it.

concepts.definition: Simple science equipment used to precisely measure the mass of an object.
concepts.key_fact: A balance compares the mass of two objects, while a scale measures the downward force to find mass.

templates: What scientific tools are officially defined as {noun_phrase}?
Which general equipment is used in the lab as {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: equipment designed to measure the total mass of an object
tools heavily used to find out exactly how much matter an object contains

concepts.rationale: Balances and scales are the standard laboratory instruments required to determine the mass or weight of solid matter.

concepts.definition: The standard unit used for measuring very light objects.
concepts.key_fact: A small grain of sand or a tiny pill is usually measured in milligrams.

templates: What is the correct base unit required when {verb_phrase}?
Scientists use what specific mass unit for {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: measuring extremely light objects like a single grain of sand
recording the exact mass of tiny, feather-light medical pills

concepts.rationale: A milligram (mg) is 1/1000th of a gram, making it the appropriate metric unit for microscopic or highly precise weights like medicine.

concepts.definition: The standard unit used for moderately light objects.
concepts.key_fact: A standard metal paperclip weighs about one gram.

templates: What is the standard metric unit applied when {verb_phrase}?
When {verb_phrase}, what is the most appropriate unit?

verb_phrase: measuring moderately light objects like an apple or a pencil
recording the physical mass of small, everyday handheld items

concepts.rationale: Grams (g) are the base metric unit for mass, ideal for measuring standard household or desktop items.

concepts.definition: The standard unit used for measuring heavier objects.
concepts.key_fact: Human body weight and large sacks of rice are typically measured in kilograms.

templates: What is the standard metric unit selected when {verb_phrase}?
When {verb_phrase}, what is the best unit to properly use?

verb_phrase: measuring much heavier objects like a person or a large desk
recording the dense mass of large, heavy everyday things

concepts.rationale: A kilogram (kg) is equal to 1,000 grams and is the standard metric unit for expressing large amounts of mass.

concepts.definition: Understanding that larger objects do not always have more mass than smaller ones.
concepts.key_fact: A small block of iron has much more mass than a large balloon filled with air.

templates: What is the analytical process of {verb_phrase}?
Which scientific term accurately describes the action of {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: determining which object has more mass even if their physical sizes differ completely
figuring out that a larger overall size doesn't always mean a heavier weight

concepts.rationale: Comparing mass teaches students the concept of density—that mass is about the amount of matter packed inside, not just the ballooning physical size.

concepts.definition: A measure of how hot or cold matter is.
concepts.key_fact: Temperature actually tells us how fast the tiny particles inside an object are moving.

templates: What do we globally call the {noun_phrase}?
Which scientific term primarily describes a {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: scientific measure of exactly how hot or cold matter is
numerical reading of the average heat energy inside an object

concepts.rationale: Temperature is the measurement of the average kinetic energy (movement) of the particles within a substance.

concepts.definition: The scientific tool used to precisely measure temperature.
concepts.key_fact: Many traditional thermometers use a special liquid that expands when it gets hot.

templates: Which specific scientific tools are universally defined as {noun_phrase}?
What is the official name of the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: tool exclusively used to measure temperature accurately
instruments designed specifically to read how hot or cold a substance is

concepts.rationale: A thermometer utilizes thermal expansion (of liquid or metal) to visually represent the kinetic energy of the substance it is touching.

concepts.definition: The appropriate unit for measuring temperature in simple science activities.
concepts.key_fact: On this scale, water freezes at 0°C and boils at 100°C.

templates: What is the standard laboratory unit recorded when {verb_phrase}?
Scientists predominantly use what specific unit for {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: recording the exact temperature in simple science class activities
measuring exactly how hot water is in the lab environment

concepts.rationale: Degrees Centigrade (Celsius) is the standard metric scale for temperature, based logically on the freezing (0) and boiling (100) points of water.

concepts.definition: Understanding how the liquid inside rises with heat and falls with cold.
concepts.key_fact: When particles get hot, they spread out, pushing the liquid up the thermometer tube.

templates: What laboratory skill actively involves {verb_phrase}?
The visual process of actively {verb_phrase} is called what?

verb_phrase: understanding exactly how the liquid inside a glass tube rises with heat
looking at the top line of liquid to objectively see how hot or cold it is

concepts.rationale: Reading a thermometer requires understanding the scale on the instrument and properly observing where the thermally expanded fluid aligns with that scale.

concepts.definition: How to properly hold and use fragile measuring equipment.
concepts.key_fact: Thermometers and glass beakers can shatter easily, so they must be placed securely on flat surfaces.

templates: What broad laboratory concept emphasizes {verb_phrase}?
Scientists must fiercely practice what overarching rule when {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: properly holding and securely using fragile measuring equipment
ensuring glass tools do not break or cause accidental injuries

concepts.rationale: Safe handling prevents dangerous accidents, such as shattering glass or spilling toxic/hot substances, ensuring a secure laboratory environment.

concepts.definition: How an increase in heat causes matter to change form.
concepts.key_fact: Adding heat makes particles move faster and spread further apart, causing the state of matter to change.

templates: What is the scientific result of the {noun_phrase}?
Which macro concept best explains the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: fact that an increase in heat can cause matter to change its physical form
process of adding intense heat energy to make matter abruptly change its state

concepts.rationale: A state change occurs when thermal energy breaks the bonds holding particles in their current phase, shifting them from solid to liquid, or liquid to gas.

concepts.definition: The process of a solid changing into a liquid.
concepts.key_fact: This happens when solid particles gain enough heat energy to break out of their tightly packed positions.

templates: What do we officially call the exact process of the {noun_phrase}?
Which scientific term perfectly describes the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: event of a solid visibly changing into a liquid
action of matter fully transforming from a rigid solid state to a flowing liquid state

concepts.rationale: Melting is the specific phase transition from a solid to a liquid, caused by an increase in internal thermal energy.

concepts.definition: The specific temperature at which a solid begins to melt.
concepts.key_fact: The melting point of pure water ice is exactly 0°C.

templates: What is the designated term for the {noun_phrase}?
Which exact measurement definitively identifies the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: specific recorded temperature at which a solid begins to physically melt
exact degree of heat needed to turn a solid chunk into a liquid puddle

concepts.rationale: Every substance has a unique melting point, the exact thermal threshold where solid bonds are broken and liquid flow begins.

concepts.definition: Real-world observations like ice turning to water or butter melting.
concepts.key_fact: We see this change every day when we leave a solid object like a chocolate bar out in the hot sun.

templates: Ice turning into water and butter softening in a hot pan are standard {noun_phrase}?
Which overarching concept is demonstrated strongly by {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: real-world visual observations of a solid actively turning into a liquid
everyday kitchen events like solid ice cream rapidly dripping on a hot day

concepts.rationale: Identifying real-world occurrences of melting helps anchor the abstract scientific concept to everyday observable reality.

concepts.definition: The process of a liquid changing into a gas.
concepts.key_fact: Evaporation can happen at any temperature, usually occurring right at the surface of the liquid.

templates: What is the official scientific process of the {noun_phrase}?
Which distinct term technically defines the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: event of a liquid naturally changing into a gas
occurrence of liquid water slowly turning into invisible water vapor in the air

concepts.rationale: Evaporation is a surface-level phase transition where liquid molecules gain enough energy to escape into the gaseous phase.

concepts.definition: Rapid evaporation that occurs when a liquid is heated to its boiling point.
concepts.key_fact: When water reaches 100°C, bubbles of gas form throughout the entire liquid, not just at the surface.

templates: What is the proper name for the {noun_phrase}?
Which scientific term accurately means the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: rapid interior evaporation that occurs when a liquid is heated to its maximum point
fast violent change from liquid to gas causing gas bubbles to form everywhere

concepts.rationale: Unlike evaporation which happens only at the surface, boiling occurs throughout the entire bulk of the liquid when it hits its thermal limit.

concepts.definition: Real-world observations like drying wet clothes or puddles disappearing.
concepts.key_fact: Heat from the sun gives the liquid water energy to turn into an invisible gas and float away.

templates: Wet clothes safely drying on a line and puddles naturally disappearing in the sun are {noun_phrase}?
Drying your wet hair with a hot hairdryer is one of many {noun_phrase}.

noun_phrase: real-world visual observations of physical liquids turning into gases
everyday normal events where water seems to disappear into the invisible air

concepts.rationale: Drying is the most common real-world application of evaporation, driven by ambient heat and air flow.

concepts.definition: The type of energy that makes particles move faster and spread apart.
concepts.key_fact: The more of this energy you add to a substance, the faster and farther apart its particles will go.

templates: What specific force is directly responsible for {verb_phrase}?
Which scientific term primarily describes the energy required for {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: making atomic particles naturally move much faster and spread apart
causing the particles inside a solid object to vividly break free and move rapidly

concepts.rationale: Heat energy (thermal energy) transfers kinetic power to particles, directly driving all 'melting' and 'evaporation' phase changes.

concepts.definition: The concept that physical changes of state can be undone by adding or removing heat.
concepts.key_fact: If you melt ice into water, you can easily reverse the process by freezing the water back into ice.

templates: What overarching concept logically describes the {noun_phrase}?
Which scientific principle powerfully states the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: fundamental idea that physical changes of matter states can be fully undone
proven ability to successfully turn a liquid back into a solid after it melts

concepts.rationale: Phase changes are physical changes, not chemical changes, meaning the original material can always be recovered by reversing the thermal energy flow.

concepts.definition: Applying melting and evaporation to accomplish daily tasks, such as cooking or drying.
concepts.key_fact: We use heat intentionally to boil water to make pasta or to use a hair dryer to dry wet hair.

templates: When we deliberately use our knowledge of science for {verb_phrase}, what are we doing?
Which broad practical concept intentionally involves {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: applying processes of melting and evaporation to accomplish daily human tasks
using heat energy intentionally to cook raw food or dry wet things safely

concepts.rationale: Human engineering relies heavily on manipulating the states of matter via heat to create tools, cook food, and improve sanitation.

concepts.definition: How a decrease in heat causes matter to change form.
concepts.key_fact: When heat is removed from a substance, it cools down and can change into a more solid state.

templates: What is the ultimate scientific result of the {noun_phrase}?
Which core concept fully explains the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: fact that a drastic decrease in heat causes matter to change form
process of deliberately removing heat energy to make matter radically change its state

concepts.rationale: Removing thermal energy forces particles to slow down and bond together, changing the state from gas to liquid, or liquid to solid.

concepts.definition: The process of a liquid changing into a solid.
concepts.key_fact: This happens when liquid particles lose enough heat energy that they stop sliding and lock into place.

templates: What do we officially call the exact process of the {noun_phrase}?
Which scientific term perfectly describes the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: event of a flowing liquid safely changing into a rigid solid
action of physical matter transforming strongly from a liquid state to a solid state

concepts.rationale: Freezing is the specific phase transition from liquid to solid, caused by a critical loss of internal thermal energy.

concepts.definition: The specific temperature at which a liquid solidifies.
concepts.key_fact: For pure water, the freezing point is exactly 0°C.

templates: What is the designated term for the {noun_phrase}?
Which exact measurement definitively identifies the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: specific recorded temperature at which a liquid officially begins to turn solid
exact degree of ambient cold needed to turn a puddle of liquid into a block of solid

concepts.rationale: The freezing point is the exact thermal floor where liquid particles can no longer slide past one another and must lock into a solid grid.

concepts.definition: Real-world observations like making ice cubes or water freezing in winter.
concepts.key_fact: We see this change when liquid water is placed into a cold freezer and turns into solid ice.

templates: Making solid ice cubes and lakes completely freezing over in winter are {noun_phrase}?
Which overarching concept is demonstrated strongly by {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: real-world visual observations of a liquid turning securely into a solid
everyday natural events like fluid water turning to solid ice on a remarkably cold night

concepts.rationale: Winter ice and kitchen freezers provide the most accessible real-world evidence of the freezing phase transition.

concepts.definition: The process of a gas changing into a liquid.
concepts.key_fact: This occurs when gas particles cool down, slow their movement, and clump together to form liquid drops.

templates: What is the official scientific process of the {noun_phrase}?
Which distinct term technically defines the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: event of a hot gas sufficiently cooling down and safely changing into a liquid
occurrence of invisible water vapor actively turning into visible liquid droplets

concepts.rationale: Condensation is the process where gaseous particles lose energy, slow down, and group together to form liquid droplets.

concepts.definition: Real-world observations like dew forming or water droplets on a cold glass.
concepts.key_fact: The water on the outside of a cold glass comes from water vapor in the air cooling down and turning liquid.

templates: Morning dew naturally forming on grass and water droplets on a cold glass are {noun_phrase}?
Seeing a bathroom mirror completely fog up after a very hot shower is one of many {noun_phrase}.

noun_phrase: real-world visual observations of floating gases turning into resting liquids
everyday normal events where water vapor forms wet liquid drops on cold flat surfaces

concepts.rationale: Condensation is commonly observed as 'sweat' on cold drinks or fog on glass, proving that the air contains invisible water vapor.

concepts.definition: Understanding that removing heat makes particles slow down and pack closer.
concepts.key_fact: As heat is lost, particles lose their speed, which makes them pack tightly together into a liquid or solid.

templates: What missing force is directly responsible for {verb_phrase}?
Which scientific term primarily describes the core reason for {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: making fast atomic particles forcibly slow down and pack much closer together
causing the chaotic particles inside a gas or liquid to heavily lose speed and clump up

concepts.rationale: Removing thermal energy steals the kinetic power of particles, allowing molecular bonds to pull them tightly into denser states of matter.

concepts.definition: How evaporation and condensation work together in nature.
concepts.key_fact: The sun evaporates water into gas, and the cold upper atmosphere condenses it back into liquid rain.

templates: What global environmental concept vividly describes the {noun_phrase}?
Which scientific phrase perfectly represents the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: massive scale of how evaporation and condensation work endlessly together in nature
continuous planetary movement of surface water turning into a gas and back into falling rain

concepts.rationale: The water cycle is the Earth's natural application of the evaporation and condensation phase changes, driven by the sun's thermal energy.

concepts.definition: Understanding melting versus freezing as reverse operations.
concepts.key_fact: Melting requires adding heat to turn a solid to liquid, while freezing requires removing heat to do the exact opposite.

templates: What is the critical analytical process of {verb_phrase}?
Which educational term technically describes the mental action of {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: understanding profoundly how solid melting and liquid freezing are completely opposite processes
analyzing precisely the difference between adding heat to purposefully melt and removing heat to heavily freeze

concepts.rationale: Comparing phase changes highlights the core principle of Reversibility in physical states based purely on energy flow.

concepts.definition: Using cooling processes to preserve food or solve daily problems.
concepts.key_fact: We put meat and vegetables in the freezer to cool them down so they last longer without spoiling.

templates: When we deliberately use our knowledge of science for {verb_phrase}, what are we formally doing?
Which broad practical concept intentionally involves {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: using mechanical cooling processes strictly to safely preserve raw food
removing internal heat intentionally to logically solve daily household problems like food going completely bad

concepts.rationale: Modern refrigeration is the pinnacle of humans applying the physics of cooling and condensation to protect the global food supply.

concepts.definition: A planned process to answer a question about the world.
concepts.key_fact: It provides a structured way to discover new facts instead of just guessing.

templates: What do we formally call the {noun_phrase}?
In structured science, the {noun_phrase} is universally known as what?

noun_phrase: highly planned process used specifically to answer a complex question about the natural world
safe, structured method deployed to accurately find out exactly how things physically work

concepts.rationale: A scientific investigation replaces random guesswork with a formal, repeatable methodology to extract absolute truth from nature.

concepts.definition: Using the senses to notice things and spark questions.
concepts.key_fact: Good scientific investigations always begin with careful observation of the surroundings.

templates: What do we officially call the foundational act of {verb_phrase}?
Which core scientific term essentially means {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: using the physical senses to quietly notice things and instantly spark new scientific questions
paying extremely close attention to worldly details purely to form a testable question

concepts.rationale: Observation is the necessary precursor to all science; you cannot test what you have not yet noticed.

concepts.definition: Identifying the specific question to answer in an experiment.
concepts.key_fact: This is the first formal step, often framed as asking, 'What is the problem?'

templates: What is the official scientific process of {verb_phrase}?
Which early scientific step heavily involves {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: identifying cleanly the highly specific question to answer formally in an upcoming experiment
figuring out exactly and precisely what unknown issue you passionately want to solve

concepts.rationale: Formulating the problem narrows a broad observation down into a single, testable, and targeted question.

concepts.definition: Making an educated guess about what the answer to the problem will be.
concepts.key_fact: A hypothesis is written before the experiment starts and can be proven right or wrong.

templates: What is the precise laboratory term for {verb_phrase}?
Which vital planning step requires {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: making an academically educated guess logically about what the final answer will be
predicting scientifically the exact outcome of a lab experiment before physically testing it

concepts.rationale: A hypothesis sets a measurable expectation. Without it, the experiment has no stated baseline to prove or disprove.

concepts.definition: Listing everything required for the experiment.
concepts.key_fact: It answers the question, 'What materials do you need?' so nothing is forgotten during the test.

templates: What necessary preparation process explicitly involves {verb_phrase}?
Which planning step practically ensures you are accurately {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: listing down systematically everything chemically required for the upcoming experiment
writing down heavily all the physical lab supplies strictly needed before safely starting

concepts.rationale: Identifying materials ensures logistical readiness so that an active chemical process isn't interrupted by missing supplies.

concepts.definition: Choosing the right tools like balances or thermometers for the investigation.
concepts.key_fact: Using the wrong tool can lead to inaccurate measurements and incorrect conclusions.

templates: What do we specifically call the technical step of {verb_phrase}?
Which vital planning step focuses heavily on {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: choosing safely the absolute right physical tools like lab balances or accurate thermometers
picking deliberately the precisely correct scientific measuring devices specifically for the test

concepts.rationale: Selecting equipment dictates the accuracy of the data. Measuring liquid volume with a beaker instead of a graduated cylinder ruins the precision.

concepts.definition: Understanding that things can change in an experiment.
concepts.key_fact: A good experiment usually only changes one variable at a time to see exactly what happens.

templates: What is the official plural term for the {noun_phrase}?
Which word universally defines the {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: environmental things or chemical states that can actively change in an experiment
environmental factors that can be safely adjusted or physically altered during a lab test

concepts.rationale: Variables are the shifting parts of an experiment. Controlling them is the only way to isolate what is actually causing an effect.

concepts.definition: Making sure an experiment is conducted evenly so results are trustworthy.
concepts.key_fact: If a test is not fair, the scientist cannot trust that their conclusion is correct.

templates: What stringent laboratory concept ensures {verb_phrase}?
Which major scientific principle focuses intensely on {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: making absolutely sure an experiment is conducted perfectly evenly so final results are undeniably trustworthy
keeping strictly all background conditions perfectly equal simply to get undeniably accurate results

concepts.rationale: Fair testing requires establishing controls (constants) to ensure the only thing causing a result is the single variable being tested.

concepts.definition: Writing down the steps before beginning the activity.
concepts.key_fact: A clear plan helps scientists stay organized and safe during the experiment.

templates: What do we broadly call the preventative act of {verb_phrase}?
Which necessary preparatory process directly involves {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: writing down fully all the safety steps comprehensively before physically beginning the lab activity
creating diligently a highly detailed manual guide to rigidly follow safely during the experiment

concepts.rationale: Investigation planning establishes the procedure, ensuring safety, repeatability, and order during what could be a chaotic physical test.

concepts.definition: Planning an investigation to answer a specific question, like 'Do gases have mass?'
concepts.key_fact: Practicing with a sample plan helps students learn the correct format before doing their own.

templates: What is the educational purpose of using a {noun_phrase}?
Which teaching term accurately describes a {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: written practice plan logically used to answer a specific scientific question like 'Do gases have mass?'
safe pre-made lab guide cleverly used to safely learn how complex experiments are designed

concepts.rationale: A sample plan offers a low-stakes framework for students to learn the methodology of science before they attempt original research.

lessons.title: The Scientific Investigation - Execution and Conclusion

concepts.definition: Following the planned steps carefully to conduct the experiment.
concepts.key_fact: Carefully following the steps ensures the experiment is conducted fairly and safely.

templates: What do we properly call the active act of {verb_phrase}?
Which post-planning term practically describes safely {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: following strictly the pre-planned steps carefully during an active live experiment
carrying out exactly and physically the written steps established in the investigation plan

concepts.rationale: Execution bridges the gap between theoretical planning and physical data collection, requiring discipline and safety awareness.

concepts.definition: Paying close attention while the experiment happens.
concepts.key_fact: Scientists use their senses to notice any changes, like bubbles forming or colors shifting, while the test is running.

templates: What is the formal process of keenly {verb_phrase}?
Which action-oriented term technically means {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: paying remarkably close sensory attention specifically while the physical experiment is actually happening
watching exceptionally carefully for any sudden physical chemical changes safely during a test

concepts.rationale: In-moment observation allows scientists to catch secondary reactions or procedural errors that numerical data alone might miss.

concepts.definition: Writing down measurements accurately as they occur.
concepts.key_fact: Data must be written down immediately so it is not forgotten or guessed later.

templates: What is the required laboratory term for explicitly {verb_phrase}?
Which highly critical step logically involves {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: writing down heavily exact factual measurements perfectly as they naturally occur
documenting legally exact scientific numbers like hot temperature or heavy mass safely during a test

concepts.rationale: Immediate recording of data removes human memory errors and ensures the statistical integrity of the entire investigation.

concepts.definition: Following rules to ensure no one gets hurt during the investigation.
concepts.key_fact: Wearing safety goggles and handling glass carefully are common safety protocols in a science lab.

templates: What do we universally call the strict practice of cautiously {verb_phrase}?
Which primary concept rigorously ensures you are appropriately {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: following strict lab rules to fully ensure absolutely no one gets horribly hurt during the investigation
obeying rigid institutional guidelines strictly designed to keep all active students totally safe in the lab

concepts.rationale: Safety protocols protect human health. A compromised lab environment halts all scientific progress.

concepts.definition: Looking at the recorded data to see what it means.
concepts.key_fact: This step turns raw numbers into useful information that helps answer the original question.

templates: What is the mathematical process of intelligently {verb_phrase}?
Which late-stage scientific step fundamentally involves {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: looking deeply at the heavily recorded data cleanly to see what it actually technically means
analyzing powerfully the raw numbers painstakingly gathered from a lab test to securely find a pattern

concepts.rationale: Data is useless without interpretation. This step converts numerical spreadsheets into recognizable scientific truths.

concepts.definition: Summarizing the results to state what was found out or learned.
concepts.key_fact: A good conclusion always refers back to the data to support its final claim.

templates: What is the structural term for properly {verb_phrase}?
Which final mandatory step requires beautifully {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: summarizing accurately all the results simply to proudly state what you have ultimately found out or learned
writing conclusively a final academic statement based entirely on the hard evidence from the experiment

concepts.rationale: The conclusion provides the final, scientifically backed answer to the question asked in step one, firmly anchored in the data collected.

concepts.definition: Checking if the initial educated guess was correct or incorrect.
concepts.key_fact: It is perfectly okay in science if your hypothesis is wrong; a wrong guess still teaches you something new.

templates: What concept strictly ensures actively {verb_phrase}?
Which part of the final conclusion focuses primarily on heavily {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: checking honestly if the very initial educated guess was ultimately correct or terribly incorrect
comparing objectively the final verified results solidly back to the very original unproven prediction

concepts.rationale: Validation checks the integrity of the original prediction. Disproving a hypothesis is just as scientifically valuable as proving it.

concepts.definition: Sharing the results of the investigation with others.
concepts.key_fact: Scientists share their findings so other people can learn from them or try the experiment themselves.

templates: What do we socially call the vital act of freely {verb_phrase}?
Which important post-experiment academic process deliberately involves smoothly {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: sharing publicly the honest results of the scientific investigation safely with others
presenting loudly what was fundamentally learned from a lab experiment to a much wider audience

concepts.rationale: Science is a communal effort. By communicating findings, scientists allow peers to peer-review their work and build upon their discoveries.

concepts.definition: Applying what was learned to situations at home or in the classroom.
concepts.key_fact: Science is most useful when the discoveries help make everyday life safer or easier.

templates: What is the global term for usefully {verb_phrase}?
Which impactful phrase totally defines brilliantly {verb_phrase}?

verb_phrase: applying heavily exactly what was learned in a strict lab strictly to situations at home or in the classroom
using confirmed scientific discoveries cleverly to logically fix massive everyday human issues

concepts.rationale: The ultimate goal of scientific discovery is the practical application of new knowledge to elevate technology and human living standards.

concepts.definition: Carrying out the plan to answer a specific question, like 'Do liquids have mass?'
concepts.key_fact: Following a sample execution helps students practice their lab skills before designing their own complex experiments.

templates: What is the educational purpose of heavily utilizing a {noun_phrase}?
Which academic term perfectly describes a {noun_phrase}?

noun_phrase: process of carrying out safely a practice plan physically to answer a question like 'Do liquids have mass?'
act of performing strongly a fully guided live experiment simply to safely build raw lab skills

concepts.rationale: A sample execution provides a safe, guided environment to practice the physical motor skills needed for complex lab tests.
